title: The Pillay Family Trail: Wey Barge Builders
area: River Wey towpath & Dapdune Wharf, Guildford
blurb: Follow the old working river right into the middle of Guildford! Hundreds of years ago, giant wooden barges were built here to carry cargo up to London. Hunt for a hamster-wheel crane, a watery boat-lift and a famous barge that sank and was found again. Flat, buggy-friendly towpath with shady riverside trees — Isaac cracks the clues, Eli spots boats and ducks. ~2.6 km, flexes 45–90 min.
difficulty: family · ages 2 & 6 · flat riverside towpath · buggy-friendly · flexes 45–90 min

start.name: Dapdune Wharf, Wharf Road (GU1 4RR)
start.directions: Central Guildford, ~5–10 min from home. The flat towpath runs SOUTH from Dapdune Wharf into town — buggy-perfect, with shady trees along the river. ⚠️ Dapdune Wharf is National Trust: the Reliance barge and barge shed are INSIDE and only open in NT hours (usually ~10:30–17:00; free for members) — for an early start, enjoy the wharf from the towpath, and the rest of the trail is free and always open. Hold little hands near the water and the lock gates. Bring (duck-friendly) snacks!

stops.name: Dapdune Wharf & the Reliance
stops.clue: This is Dapdune Wharf — where workers BUILT eleven giant wooden barges to carry cargo up and down the river! A famous barge called the Reliance lives here. (If the wharf gate is open, climb aboard and say hello.) Get to the wharf and check in.
stops.little_ones: Eli — can you find a boat on the water and quack at a duck? 🦆
stops.grownup: Dapdune Wharf (National Trust): 11 Wey barges were built here. The barge 'Reliance' was built in 1932, sank under London's Cannon Street Bridge in 1968, was found 21 years later in Essex, restored, and now sits in dry dock here.
stops.story: Dapdune Wharf is where they built the big barges. The Reliance carried goods to London until she sank in 1968 — then was lost for 21 years before being found and brought home!

stops.name: The River That Worked
stops.clue: This isn't just any river — long ago people turned the River Wey into a 'navigation' so heavy boats could travel on it, one of the very FIRST in the whole country. Was it opened in 1653, 1953, or last week?
stops.answer_hint: It's REALLY old — over 350 years ago. The oldest of the three.
stops.little_ones: Eli — drop a leaf or a twig in the water and watch it float away downstream! 🍃
stops.grownup: The Wey Navigation opened in 1653 — one of the first river navigations in England — built by Sir Richard Weston with 12 locks so barges could reach the Thames.
stops.story: The River Wey was made into a 'navigation' way back in 1653 so heavy barges could float all the way to London. One of the oldest in England!

stops.name: The Hamster-Wheel Crane
stops.clue: By the river in town stands a GIANT old crane — and people powered it by walking inside a huge wheel, just like a hamster! It lifted heavy cargo off the barges. Find it. What is the great wheel made of: wood, plastic, or gold?
stops.answer_hint: It's old and brown and creaky — it grows on trees!
stops.little_ones: Eli — pretend YOU'RE inside the big wheel: march, march, march on the spot! 🐹
stops.grownup: The Town Wharf treadwheel crane is a rare survivor — a human-powered (treadwheel) crane that unloaded barges at Guildford's wharf, built of timber and kept as a monument.
stops.story: This wooden crane was powered by PEOPLE walking inside the big wheel to lift heavy loads off the boats — no engines needed!

stops.name: Millmead Lock
stops.clue: Boats can't climb a step in the river — so clever people built a LOCK, a watery lift! Big wooden gates trap water to raise a boat up, then let it out to lower it down. Find the lock near the town mill and read its name sign. What is this lock called?
stops.answer_hint: It's named after the old town MILL nearby… Mill-something.
stops.little_ones: Eli — watch the water pour through the gates, and find the big wooden lever that swings them open! 🔧
stops.grownup: Millmead Lock sits beside Millmead in central Guildford on the 1653 Wey Navigation — one of the 12 locks lifting boats up the river. The big timber 'balance beams' swing the gates open.
stops.story: A lock is a watery lift for boats! Gates fill a chamber with water to raise a barge, then empty it to lower one. This one's called Millmead Lock.

stops.name: The Town Bridge
stops.clue: You've reached Guildford's old stone Town Bridge over the river. Long ago, wooden bridges here kept getting washed away by floods! Stand on or by the bridge and check in, river crew. Keep an eye out for the little electric barge that gives people rides.
stops.little_ones: Eli — wave at anyone on a boat, and count the arches under the bridge: one… two…! 🌉
stops.grownup: Guildford's Town Bridge crosses the Wey in the heart of town; earlier timber bridges here were repeatedly wrecked by floods (notably 1900) before today's stone crossing.
stops.story: This is the Town Bridge, right in the middle of Guildford. The old wooden bridges kept washing away in floods — so now it's strong stone!

finale.title: Barge Builders Isaac & Eli — cargo delivered! 🚤🎉
finale.message: You found the wharf and the Reliance, cracked the river's 1653 secret, powered the hamster-wheel crane, named Millmead Lock and reached the Town Bridge. Top river crew! Show Mum and Dad all your stickers.
finale.reward_idea: A riverside ice cream, or a hot chocolate in town — captain's orders! 🍦

credits: Map data © OpenStreetMap contributors · facts © Wikipedia (CC BY-SA) / National Trust
_recce_notes: PREP 2026-06-20, close-to-home option. Start Dapdune Wharf GU1 4RR (~51.2426,-0.5778; NT — interior open NT hours only, towpath free/always open). Linear flat towpath S into town, ~1.2 km each way / ~2.6 km round, ~60–90 min with the boys, buggy-friendly. SOFT pins to FOOT-VERIFY + retune geofences: treadwheel crane (stop 3, ~51.2362,-0.5746 — confirm exact spot + that it's signed), Millmead Lock (stop 4, ~51.2335,-0.5728 — confirm the name sign), Town Bridge (5); stops 1–2 wharf/towpath approximate. Open riverside = decent GPS. Durable clues: treadwheel crane (wood) + Millmead Lock name; the rest virtual/arrival since the NT wharf interior may be shut early. Verify the crane's current location/interpretation board.
